FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; N=7,514; ages 9-10 at baseline 2016-2018; 21 research sites across the U.S. (Barch et al., 2018)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis; Years 1-3; modified Poisson regression with mediation analysis using GSEM

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Substance Use & Misuse, 2024; no preprint/duplicate identified

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time (self-reported hours per day, Year 1) is qualifying SM-use IV; alcohol sipping (Timeline Follow-Back, Year 3) is individual-trait health outcome (substance use initiation); directional pairing as IV→DV

FT_IC3.evidence: RR 1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43) p<0.001 for social media time predicting alcohol sipping; RR 1.21 (95% CI 1.10-1.33) p<0.001 when including mediator

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: direct effect β=0.03 (95% CI 0.01-0.05) p=0.001; indirect effect β=0.011 (BC 95% CI 0.007-0.016) p<0.001; proportion mediated 25.0% (95% CI 11.0-39.3) p=0.001

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal but SM (social media time, Year 1) appears as IV in main analysis predicting sipping (Year 3); SM-as-IV role disqualifies EC7 exclusion
notes: Strong prospective design with mediation analysis; qualifying SM exposure and individual-trait health outcome

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; N=4,718; ages 10-15 years (2018-2021); longitudinal data from Year 2 and Year 3

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional data from Year 2-3; linear and multivariate logistic regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in BMC Public Health, 2024; no preprint/duplicate identified

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as multi-device aggregated "recreational screen time hours per day" including television, movies, video chat, video games, texting, videos, social media with no per-device breakdown isolating smartphone or SM. Per methods: "Self-reported recreational screen time hours per day were classified as low (0-4), medium (>4-8), and high (>8) hours per day." This is composite bundled screen time without isolation of phone/SM as required by FT-IC2

notes: Fails FT-IC2 because screen time is generic multi-device aggregate without per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; N=9,538; ages 9-10 at baseline 2016-2018; longitudinal with two-year follow-up to Year 2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis; mixed-effects models with random effects examining baseline screen time predicting follow-up mental health symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in BMC Public Health, 2024; no preprint/duplicate identified

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary independent variable is "total screen time" (composite of television, movies, video chat, video games, texting, videos, and social media) measured as aggregate hours per day with no per-modality breakdown isolating smartphone or social media use. Methods state weighted mean total screen time calculated "summing all types of media" with no per-device isolation. Although paper reports associations with specific screen types post-hoc, the primary analysis uses bundled composite screen time without isolation of phone/SM construct, violating FT-IC2

notes: Fails FT-IC2 because total screen time is composite bundled measure without per-modality isolation of smartphone or social media; post-hoc stratification by screen type does not remedy primary-analysis failure
